entry.title: From Application Logging to Cloud Logging: Service Innovation Guide
entry.content:
This service innovation guide explains the innovation journey from SAP Application Logging Service towards SAP Cloud Logging. It shall help customers to understand the benefits and differences to make best possible use of SAP Cloud Logging and to prepare for the migration from SAP Application Logging. It is complemented by a service migration guide describing a procedure to switch from the Application Logging to the Cloud Logging.

Motivation

SAP Application Logging Service successfully served Cloud Foundry application developers for almost 10 years to store, visualize, and analyze their log messages. To improve its usability even further we repeatedly received some key requirements from customers, incl. alerting, configurable retention periods, custom dashboards, and many more. Technical, security, privacy, and compliance restrictions did not allow us to implement these requirements on the underlying multi-tenant stack.

That’s why we created a completely new solution, SAP Cloud Logging. It provides each customer with a dedicated and fully isolated observability stack. This allowed us to not only implement the above-listed requirements but also to innovate by supporting metrics & traces (via OpenTelemetry) and serving major BTP runtimes (Cloud Foundry, Kyma, and others).

SAP Cloud Logging has been proven to be a successful and reliable service, which is why we will deprecate SAP Application Logging Service. This service innovation guide is designed to help you to understand the full potential of SAP Cloud Logging, including its additional benefits, while highlighting the areas where it behaves differently or requires different setup and usage considerations. 

Capabilities & Differences

SAP Cloud Logging comes with many advanced capabilities beyond those of SAP Application Logging Service. The most relevant ones are listed below:

While almost all feature improvements can be simply consumed on top of the existing experience there are 2 features worth mentioned explicitly:

The authentication system for Cloud Logging has to cover a much more general scenario as it is not only bound to Cloud Foundry. The resulting setup via SAML/IDP requires a one-time setup effort on the customer side compared to the Cloud Foundry-centric setup in Application Logging. We provide detailed documentation on how this can be done. This makes the service future proof to support all SAP runtimes.

  • As of today, there is one minor feature gap which is the exposure of Cloud Foundry container metrics for Cloud Foundry applications. This gap shall be closed by soon.
  • Dashboard:
    • The “Statistics” dashboard about quota-based logs dropping is not provided for Cloud Logging Service because it does not drop logs based on quota
    • The “Metrics” dashboard will be part of the CF container metrics rollout

Service Deployment

Regional Hub

Application Logging is deployed in every Cloud Foundry landscape.

Cloud Logging is a generalized offering that can be jointly used across runtimes, environments, and even data centers. Therefore, it’s deployed once per (compliance) region, from where it’s offered for consumption in all related BTP landscapes and can be commonly used.

 

Cross tenant (CF Space) analytics

Application Logging is a shared stack where authorization is restricted based on tenant (CF Space) access. For a user, who has CF Space Developer role in multiple spaces, this allows “joint log analysis” across all logging service instances from those multiple spaces within that BTP landscape.

As Cloud Logging is built on dedicated stacks for each service instance, it does not offer joint analysis across those service instances. However, as Cloud Logging enables sharing beyond a single space, you could setup single instance to serve apps from multiple spaces, thereby achieving joint analytics. Also note, CLS also offers fine-grained user authorization – so within a single instance you could define different access rule for your users (restrict space-based access to logs, read only, edit dashboard, hide sensitive logs etc.).

Landscape availability

The availability of Cloud Logging in certain BTP landscapes may shortly follow the initial launch of the landscape. Currently, this is the case for BTP in Switzerland (CH20).

Improved Ingestion Security

Securing Ingestion and rotation

Since ingestion to Cloud Logging is flexible and in customers’ control, it is necessary to offer industry standard ingestion authentication. OTel and ingest endpoints support mTLS ingestion which help to comply with most organization’s security standards.

With certificate-based security mechanism users are responsible for timely rotation of certificate to avoid expiration. With just regular re-binding, Cloud logging allows renewing client certificates. Please read the document for best practice on rotation without disruption.

User provided services

We also recommend leveraging User provided service which offers much faster and effective binding in following cases:

  • If your deployment does frequent bind/unbind but you do not intend to rotate certificates, then you could create a User provided service based on the Cloud Logging instance’s service key. As the actual bind/unbind is an expensive and time-consuming process involving new certificate generation, this approach w/ UPS makes the binding faster, fail safe and uninterrupted. Also minimizes the number of certificates that needs to be monitored and rotated. When it is actually needed to rotate the certificates, you could create an additional service key, update the UPS and rebind workloads.
  • When you are using central CLS instance across different sub-accounts / BTP landscapes, then UPS is way to reuse the existing instance in one location across many.
  • Of course, this requires keeping the UPS manually up-to date with the service keys.

Service Plans & Pricing

Application Logging comes with fixed-size service plans charged monthly. In contrast to this, the flexible configuration and auto-scaling of Cloud Logging comes with a more fine-grained approach.

Service Plans

Application Logging service plans are designed based on ingestion quota. Cloud Logging offers service plans along the number of provisioned resources.

Cloud Logging offers

  • 2 production-ready service plans (standard & large) which offer net storage capacity from 75 GB to 3.75 TB.
  • 1 development plan (dev) which offers all functional features of production plans but lacks cloud qualities, i.e. no data replication, no auto-scaling, and very limited ingestion throughput. è It’s for evaluation purposes only and must not be used in production use cases.
  • its development plan also in the context of SAP Build Code

While Application Logging offers a specific lite plan for evaluation purposes at no charge cost there is no comparable offering in Cloud Logging. This is due to the dedicated provisioning of resources per Cloud Logging instance. Furthermore, customers may reduce cost for development scenarios by sharing Cloud Logging service instances across stakeholders and scenarios.

Pricing

In contrast to the fixed-size and full-month approach of Application Logging the pricing approach of Cloud Logging works as follows:

  • Metering & pricing is based on provisioned components (foundation, storage, ingestion).
  • Autoscaling of underlying components is explicitly captured.
  • Metering is done ”hourly”, i.e. is based on what is physically deployed at the metered hour.
  • Usage is aggregated into a single metric: “capacity unit”.

The benefits of this approach are metering is highly accurate to actual consumption per hour and selection of service plans incl. specification of upper bounds for auto-scaling components ensures cost planning safety while still allowing for flexible adaption for changes in observability load.

Example: If customers have configured to use autoscaling for ingest (say 10 blocks) and has been using all 10. At a certain hour when there is no ingest, they will scale-in to 2 and be billed for 2 blocks for that hour. (same applies to disk/storage). Note: There is no scale to 0.
So, if users create an instance with min configuration of standard plan (without auto-scaling) - they would’ve consumed 538 Capacity Units after “30 days” (lower for 28 day months and slightly higher for 31 day months).

Activity shorter than a month (mostly in evaluation cases or cases where instances are created & destroyed often): If the instance is created on 5th morning at 9:00 AM à evaluated until 6th evening 16:00 PM and deprovisioned – then the billing is only for the 31 hours instead of the complete month. On the other hand, this is different from Application logging, where it is billed for complete month for each short incarnation of the service’s instances. i.e., each service “recreate” will be fully billed for a month.

Use SAP Cloud Logging cost estimator to determine cost based on your needs.

Eventual billing numbers are reported via a specific service plan, called “overall”, which is used to aggregate billing numbers across all actual service plans (standard, large, dev). Metrics captured on detailed metrics (e.g. number of ingest blocks) can be inspected via Cloud Cockpit.

Sizing

This section discusses how many SAP Cloud Logging instances you need, and how many apps to bind to one instance. We recommend revisiting which apps are bound to which logging service plan instance and not having a one-to-one mapping of service plans. Compared to Application Logging, Cloud Logging instances can handle significantly more log volume. For most use cases, peak ingestion performance should not be a problem for either of the services.

What previously has been sent to multiple service instances can now be sent to one. This solution allows for additional analyses and can be the more cost-efficient option.

For Application Logging, log ingestion is limited by the service plan quota limit, which defines the theoretical maximum of log storage. Cloud Logging instances have auto-scaling capabilities and a pay-per-resource usage model in which it checks usage hourly.

To determine the storage demand, check the statistics dashboard in Application Logging on how many logs (and bytes) you are shipping and how many have been dropped. When calculating the storage demand, consider the data retention. While data retention is 7 days for Application Logging, it is configurable in Cloud Logging Service. An increase in data retention increases your storage demand. For example, increasing retention from 7 to 30 days increases storage demand by the factor of 4.28.

If you have multiple applications forming a single solution and the combined log volume fits a single Cloud Logging instance, we recommend binding them all to that instance. This allows you to analyze your application data together and investigate cross-application problems.

Find more detailed information on sizing, performance, and Cloud Logging Service plans at the Discovery Center.

Instance Planning

While the setup and usage of Application Logging is always confined by a Cloud Foundry landscape Cloud Logging offers way more flexibility for re-using existing instances in multiple contexts. This comes with technical, cost, and legal implications thus requires proper planning.

Reusability / Shareability

Cloud Logging comes with the ability to “share” single instance across various application deployment locations.

  • This increases convenience of centralizing observability in a single or fewer instances.
  • This drastically reduces cost in contrast to Application Logging where customers need to create different instances per space.

Sharing across spaces:

Sharing across runtimes:

Sharing across DCs in a region:

Sharing across DCs across a region:

  • Technically it is also possible to extend the above scenario to share across regions (US <-> EU) depending on the ingest load and latency.

Tradeoffs

Planning actual instantiation and deployment of Cloud Logging service instances should consider the following aspects:

  • Cost: in most cases shared instances should be more cost effective than dedicated ones.
  • Legal/Operational aspects might enforce customers to run dedicated instances. Still Cloud Logging provides many security-level features (e.g. index-level security) which might be sufficient to satisfy those.
  • Analytics: putting observability data into a shared instances allows for combined analysis. Note: in contrast to Application Logging, data cannot be analyzed across service instances.
  • Ingestion throughput: Remote ingestion (from Cloud Foundry) may come with bit lower throughput.

Outlook

Cloud Logging service is constantly innovating to bring OpenSearch features after passing them through SAP Product standards. Few of the highlighting features which are under investigation are:

  • Migration to OpenSearch 2.x
  • OIDC based Auth in addition to SAML
  • Extra Large plan with possible tiering/snapshot
  • Multiple data sources for visualization across multiple Cloud logging instances
  • Index compression for efficient storage and performance
  • Simplified default user managements and API access
  • And many more…

Once the features pass investigation phase and committed for delivery you could find them published in the Roadmap explorer or What’s New section.

entry.title: From Application Logging to Cloud Logging: Service Migration Guide
entry.content:
This service migration guide provides a 6-step procedure with which you can smoothly setup, test, and eventually handover logging from Application Logging to Cloud Logging service.

It offers a side-by-side setup of Cloud Logging, lets data flow simultaneously into both, Application & Cloud Logging, and once you are satisfied, you can switch your observability procedures onto Cloud Logging and shutdown Application Logging.

It's complemented by a service innovation guide which helps to understand the full potential of Cloud Logging and where it differs from Application Logging.

1) Plan your Cloud Logging setup

Based on the knowledge from service Innovation guide, first plan the following:

  • How many instances?
  • How to centralize?
  • Size of each instance?
  • Who should have access to which instances?

2) Setup Identity Provider

In Cloud Logging, you have to configure your own user management (IDP). To have the same Single Sign On (SSO) experience, you must bring your own IAS account. Please refer to this guide on how to create a tenant SAML application in SAP BTP’S Identity Authentication Service (IAS).

  • SAML authentication cannot be enabled on an existing Cloud Logging Service instance with a service update. Parameters must be passed on service creation.
  • If you decide to utilize multiple CLS instances, you could use leverage the same SAML application from IAS to setup all CLS instances.

Note: Omitting the SAML configuration would fall back to Basic Authentication, which could be useful for evaluation purposes.

3) Setup Cloud Logging

Create instances of Cloud Logging Service and bind your applications based on our documentation.

  • Enter your SAML configuration as a configuration parameter derived from the previous step. You cannot enable SAML authentication on an existing Cloud Logging Service instance with a service update.
  • You have more freedom to configure, compared to Application Logging. For example, you can configure up to 90 days of retention. See Configuration Parameters to have a suitable parametrization.

4) Bind and instrument your applications

Bind your application to Cloud Logging – in parallel to the existing binding to Application Logging.

This let’s log data flow simultaneously into both, Application & Cloud Logging, so gives a smooth transition and allows to compare the results before the eventual switch.

Note: Since there is not ingestion-based quota in Cloud Logging, you might notice an increased ingestion as all logs are ingested. Application logging Statistics dashboard provides insights into the quota decisions. For each application, the aggregated message count and volume of accepted and rejected messages is reported for short time intervals. This allows determination of the total message volume, enabling an informed decision on the required service plan.

In case you use custom fields, please refer to the Annex B for instrumentation guide.

5) Validate Setup

Check if logs arrive and are parsed correctly. Remember, you have your own OpenSearch Dashboards, and your own URL which can be read from the cf service-key and that contains all endpoints and credentials.

To learn what you can do, check the using your Cloud Logging Service instance documentation and see how to ingest, alert, manage access rights, and so on.

6) Deprovision Application Logging

After at most 7 days, Cloud Logging instances hold all the logs which are in Application Logging.

So, you can unbind the applications from Application Logging instances (cf unbind-service) and delete the Application Logging service instance (cf delete-service). Existing bindings must be deleted before the instance can be deprovisioned.

 

Annex A) Process Visualization

The following visualizations explain the 6-step migration process via a concrete example on how the respective setups of application logging and cloud logging may look like.

 

Annex B) Adjust Custom Fields Configuration When Sending with Logging Libraries

This section applies only if you are sending custom fields with one of our logging libraries (Java/NodeJS). Application Logging requires configuring custom fields in the logging library, while Cloud Logging Service supports them without additional configuration.

The migration requires a configuration change to support both services simultaneously. You must send custom fields in #cf nested object and at top-level.

Node.js: The CF NodeJS logging library automatically detects which services you are bound to (Application Logging and/or Cloud Logging Service) and adjusts sending custom fields since version v6.5.0. Make sure you updated the logging library to this version or higher.

Java: You must configure the CF Java logging library, dependent on the logging backend you use.

  • For Log4j Logging Backend: Set retainOriginal to true in your log4j XML configuration file. That means replacing <customField mdcKeyName="custom-field" retainOriginal="false" /> with <customField mdcKeyName="custom-field" retainOriginal="true" /> for all custom fields.
  • For Logback Logging Backend: For each custom-field configuration line, another one has to be appended to retain that custom field at top level. For example, the <customFieldMdcKeyName>custom-field</customFieldMdcKeyName> configuration line should be followed by this one: <retainFieldMdcKeyName>custom-field</retainFieldMdcKeyName>.

After deprovisioning the Application Logging instance, you can remove all configuration regarding custom fields.

entry.title: New fields in the EFD – Reinf Report (Brazil) Migration Object
entry.content:
Hello,

Hello SAP Community,

I am excited to share some important updates with you! The EFD – Reinf report (Brazil) migration object has recently been enhanced to include new fields that will assist you in migrating your data. Each of these new fields is associated with specific EFD-Reinf events, as listed below.

For the R-1050 event:

  • Classification of the Related Entity (TP_ENT_LIG)
  • CNPJ of the Related Entity (CNPJ_LIG)

For the R-4099 event:

  • Closing or Reopening Indicative (FECH_RET)

For the R-4080 event:

  • CNPJ of the Source Paying the Income (CNPJ_FONT)

For the R-4010 event:

  •  Beneficiary's CPF (CPF_BENEF)
  • Beneficiary's Name (NM_BENEF)
  • ID of Additional File Sent by Benefic. (IDE_ADIC_BENEF)

For the R-4020 event:

  • Beneficiary's Name (NM_BENEF)
  • Beneficiary's CNPJ (CNPJ_BENEF)
  • ID of Additional File Sent by Benefic. (IDE_ADIC_BENEF)

For the R-4040 event:

  • ID of Additional File Sent by Benefic. (IDE_ADIC_BENEF)

To learn more about these updates and stay informed with our latest news, I recommend you visit the following SAP Help Portal pages:

  • About the migration object: EFD - Reinf report (Brazil)
  • EFD-Reinf Report product assistance page. 
  • Available Migration Objects

 

We would love your feedback. If you have any questions or suggestions for an upcoming post, please leave your comment below. 

See you next time!

entry.title: Design with Accessibility in Mind: SAP’s Accessibility Design Tools
entry.content:
Building products with accessibility in mind is vital to creating great user experiences. At SAP, we believe accessibility should be part of the design process from the beginning. When accessibility is considered early on, creating products that work for everyone is easier, leading to better results for users and teams.

To support designers and developers in this effort, we’ve created a set of Accessibility Design Tools. These tools make it easier to design with accessibility in mind, helping you build more inclusive products while saving time and effort along the way. Whether you’re a designer, product manager, or developer, our tools are here to help you create products that are accessible from the start.

Blue background banner with accessibility symbol representing a human figure with arms raised on the right. Symbol is hovering over a wireframe user interface mockup and sends three rays to different interface positions. Contains text “Accessibility Design Tools - Plugins and Accessibility Annotations for Designers."

The Accessibility Design Tools Handbook

One way to create products with improved accessibility is by embedding accessibility into the design process. When accessibility is considered early, teams can build more efficient workflows during the design phase, leading to a more streamlined development process.

That’s where SAP’s Accessibility Design Tools Handbook comes in. This guide empowers designers, developers, and product managers to integrate accessibility right from the start. It provides practical steps for annotating designs to meet accessibility standards, including those from the Web Content Accessibility Guidelines (WCAG), while encouraging broader considerations throughout the design process. With this resource, teams can ensure their products are designed with accessibility in mind.

The Handbook helps make accessibility a natural part of the design process, leading to more efficient development and better user experiences for all.

Infographic of various accessibility annotation elements, such as wayfinding, tooltips, error prevention, and speech output. The elements are displayed in a variety of colors and shapes.

Annotation Library

Manually annotating designs for accessibility can be time-consuming and tedious and lead to inconsistencies. To help streamline this process, we collaborated with teams across SAP to develop the Annotation Library, which provides designers with a set of annotations covering about 70% of the WCAG 2.2 success criteria. The annotations are organized across five core areas:

  • Foundation: Helps designers prepare UI components for accessible interaction and navigation, covering input and output mechanisms, consistency, and reusable component names to ensure an efficient user experience
  • Visual Design: Focuses on color contrast, themes, and sensory concepts, while ensuring UI elements behave correctly during text resizing, zooming, orientation changes, and contrast adjustments.
  • Screen Reader: Guides designers in structuring reading order, headings, and landmarks with annotations to create an intuitive experience beyond what’s visible on the screen.
  • Keyboard Support: Provides annotation for preparing screens and components for keyboard navigation, including focus order, shortcuts, and identifying initial focus and skipping groups.
  • Complementary Assets: Offers annotations for additional considerations, such as multimedia, time limits, and error prevention, to enhance accessibility across different user needs.

Figma Plugin

Annotating multiple screens and every UI element can be time-consuming. To reduce the time burden and increase efficiency, we created a Figma Plugin based on a subset of annotation assets, including:

  • Screen reading order
  • Focus order
  • Landmarks
  • Headings

The plugin is a free beta version and can be accessed on Figma Community.

Ready to Design Products with Accessibility in Mind?

By using the Annotation Library and Figma Plugin together, teams can streamline the process of integrating accessibility into their designs. These tools not only make it easier to meet accessibility standards like WCAG 2.2 but also empower designers to create more inclusive products from the start.

With these resources, accessibility becomes a natural part of the design process, leading to better user experiences and more efficient collaboration and workflows for teams.

Make accessibility an integral part of your design process:

  • Download the Accessibility Design Tools Handbook
  • Access the Annotation Library in the Figma Community
  • Try out the Accessibility Design Tools Plugin for Figma
  • Explore the Accessibility Design Tools Plugin Code available open source on GitHub

entry.title: Update on Reference Solution Architecture for SAP Master Data Governance
entry.content:
Update on Reference Solution Architecture for SAP Master Data Governance

We are excited to announce the release of the new version of the Reference Solution Architecture for Master Data Governance (MDG), which has recently been published on Business Accelerator Hub and on the SAP Signavio Process Navigator - SAP for Me.

This updated version includes new solution variants for SAP Master Data Governance on SAP S/4HANA 2023 FPS02 in classic mode and in cloud-ready mode. One of the key highlights of this release is the inclusion of detailed diagrams for business partner, product, and financial domain for master data governance in classic mode. These diagrams provide a comprehensive overview of the solution architecture, making it easier for users to understand how MDG fits into their existing IT landscape and how it can be integrated with other systems and applications.  

Updates in Detail

1. Solution Variant “Federated Master Data Governance with Core Data Owner on SAP S/4HANA”

We added the information that Federated Master Data Governance can also be used with MDG on lower releases/classic-mode. More information can be found in the how-to guide and in the blog "Consumption of Federated Governance Made Easy" from @Mark63.

In the next two solution variants, we have replaced the existing one for “Centrally Managed Master Data Governance on SAP S/4HANA” with two new solution variants to illustrate the supported processes: 

2. Solution Variant “Centrally Managed Master Data Governance on SAP S/4HANA – Classic Mode”

In the Solution Value Flow Diagram, you can see that MDG on SAP S/4HANA, classic mode supports multiple domains, including business partner, product, and financials. This diagram provides a clear representation of how a business process variant is implemented and supported by a specific solution. 

As example you can see the modelled Solution Process Flow Diagrams for Decentral Creation and Decentral Change of Business Partners. In the case of Decentral Creation, a consolidation process is used, whereas for Decentral Change, the MDG, Central Governance Change Request is used. 

The descriptive text next to the diagram contains more information: It provides details about the standard delivered process template and change request types that are suitable for decentralized creation and change processes.  

3. Solution Variant “Centrally Managed Master Data Governance on SAP S/4HANA – Cloud-Ready Mode”

In comparison to the Solution Variant for MDG on SAP S/4HANA, classic mode you can see the Solution Value Flow Diagram for cloud-ready mode currently only contains the Business Partner Domain. 

The Solution Process Flow Diagrams were initially published last year. We have now included three new diagrams, which demonstrate the support for Mass Load, Mass Change, and Mass Deletion of Business Partner Relationships. 

Furthermore, to draw a comparison with classic mode diagrams, here you can see the modelled Solution Process Flow Diagrams for Decentral Creation and Decentral Change of Business Partners in cloud-ready mode. In both cases, consolidation processes are used. 

We believe that this latest release will be a valuable resource for organizations looking to implement or expand their MDM capabilities. By providing a clear and comprehensive overview of the solution architecture, we aim to empower users to make the most of their MDM investment and to achieve their data management goals more efficiently.  

We invite you to explore the new Reference Solution Architecture for MDM by yourself on the Business Accelerator Hub and on the SAP Signavio Process Navigator - SAP for Me.

Further information

Curious about the initially released content and about the different diagram types such as Solution Value Flow diagram, Solution Component diagram, Solution Data Flow diagram, Solution Process Flow diagram? 

  • Check the blog post “Explore Reference Solution Architecture for Master Data Governance” from my colleague @KarinBlank. 
  • Watch our Video: How to use Reference Solution Architecture for SAP Master Data Governance - YouTube 

To stay up to date, simply follow the SAP Community Topic Page for SAP Master Data Governance, join the Questions & Answers forum and Blog space, and check the MDG roadmap for all MDG versions. 

Best, Christina Lindner

entry.title: End-To-End Creation of View Cluster
entry.content:
View Cluster: 

View Cluster is a group of maintenance dialogs which are collected in maintenance unit for business or technical reasons. 

The main purpose is that view cluster allows end users to maintain the complex cross-table data and ensure consistency of the data. 

SE54 is the transaction code for creating the View Cluster. 

SM34 is the transaction code to maintain entries in View Cluster. 

Let's Create View cluster using tables SCARS, SPFLI and SFLIGHT. 

First, we need to create maintenance views for tables SCARS, SPFLI and SFLIGHT. 

Create maintainace view for SCARR. 

Step 1: Go to SE11 and create a maintainace view for SCARR. 

Step 2 : Give description and table with which you want to create maintenance view. 

Step 3: Click on view table. 

Step 4 : Click on table fields and select the fields you want. 

Step 5: Click on Maintenance status and select the option you want for access and save and activate. 

Step 6: Go to Utilities and click on table maintenance generator and create a TMG for it. 

Step 7: Provide the function group, Authorization group and maintenance screens and click on create. 

So, maintenance view is created for Scarr (Airline) Table, similarly, need to create maintenance view for SPFLI and Flight table also. 

 

Note: 1. For SPFLI while creating the Maintenance view, once you select the fields from the table you will get the screen like below. 

In the tab view Fields Give ‘S’ in the column ‘P’ for the field Carrid. 

Note: 2. While creating Maintenance view for SFLIGHT 

Follow the same procedure as before for creating maintenance view for SFLIGHT. 

In view fields in the column ‘P’ provide ‘S’ for the fields CARRID AND CONNID as below. 

Once Maintenance view for all the above tables is created Goto SE54 Tcode and create the view cluster as below. 

Steps Create View cluster in SE54. 

Step 1: Goto SE54 and click on Edit View Cluster. 

Step 2: Click on view cluster name and click on Create/Change button. 

Step 3: Provide a short description and save. 

Step 4: Double Click on Object Structure in the left side. 

Step 5: Click on the New Entries button in the toolbar menu. 

Step 6: Enter View/Table, Short text and precedes, Dep,pos,Start. 

The Dep column of the view cluster explains the details of the entry. 

R = Header Entry. 

S = Dependent entry. 

The POS column explains the sequence of the views. 

Start column explains the first view in hierarchy, which is in ZVSCARR in this case. 

Enter the data shown below and click on the Enter Key. 

Step 7: Click on enter. 

Then provide the next view name ZVSPFLI and in the Dep column of it the value of ZSPFLI is ‘S’ because it is dependent on the predecessor view ZVSCARR, and value of predecessor is ZVSCARR. 

Then again do the same procedure for ZVSFLIGHT also in Dep column of it the value of ZVSFLIGHT is ‘S’ because it is dependent on the predecessor view ZVSPFLI, and value of predecessor is ZVSPFLI. 

Step 8: Now Select each line and click on the field dependent button, a popup appears saying ‘Field Dependencies successfully created’ Confirm information popup with enter key or click green tick. 

Similarly do the same things to remaining View/table also. 

Step 9: Once it is done for all the views, click on Header entry/goback to the main screen and click activate button. 

 

 Till now we created the View Cluster. 

Maintaining View Cluster in SM34 

Step 1: Now Go to SM34 and Enter view cluster name and click on Display/Maintenance button. 

In the first screen all the existing entries of the table SCARR would appear. 

First Screen. 

Select the record in the table scarr and click on table SPFLI. 

You will get all the records related to carrid AA. 

Select particular Connid from SPFLI View and click on table Flight you will get all the entries related to selected connid as below.

entry.title: Migrating SAP Smart forms to Adobe Forms
entry.content:
SAP Smart Forms and Adobe Forms are two popular technologies for designing and generating business documents like invoices, purchase orders, and delivery notes. As organizations modernize their SAP landscapes, there is a growing need to migrate from Smart Forms to Adobe Forms to leverage advanced features, better performance, and an enhanced user experience. This document gives an idea about migration of a smart form to an interactive form. 

Why Migrate to Adobe Forms?

  1. Enhanced User Experience:
    • Adobe Forms offer dynamic layouts and interactivity (e.g., input fields, drop-downs).
  2. Modern Integration:
    • Seamless integration with Fiori apps and SAP S/4HANA.
  3. Rich Features:
    • Advanced graphics, barcodes, and accessibility features.
  4. Global Standards:
    • Support for PDF/A compliance for archiving and digital signatures.
  5. Future-Proof:
    • SAP is focusing more on Adobe Forms as a long-term solution in its product roadmap.

Migration Challenges

Migrating from Smart Forms to Adobe Forms involves technical and functional considerations:

  • Layout Redesign: Smart Forms and Adobe Forms have different layout tools and paradigms.
  • Data Binding: Adobe Forms use context nodes and data connections, requiring re-mapping of data.
  • Functional Enhancements: Migrating is an opportunity to enhance existing forms with interactive features.
  • Learning Curve: Development in Adobe Forms requires knowledge of Adobe LiveCycle Designer.

Steps to Migrate

Step1: Go to transaction code SMARTFORMS and select the smart form to be converted into print adobe form. 

 

Here the form name is ZSRS_SF_TEST. 

Step2: Go to the next screen by clicking display button. 

A text element and table are defined in this existing smart form. 

Step3: Output of the smart form ZSRS_SF_TEST is:

Step4: Now go to Utilities-àMigration--àInteractive Form-àExport in the initial screen of the smart form as shown below. 

You will get popup. 

Step5: Provide form name and interface name and click enter. 

Step6: You will get settings window as shown below. 

Select the necessary check boxes. 

Select ‘OK’ button. It asks for package and provides the package. Save it.

Step7: Now go adobe form initial screen Tcode SFP. Provide the interface name as mentioned above.  

Step8: Go to properties tab. You get the default interface type is ‘Smart forms-Compatible Interface’ as shown. 

Activate it.

Activate the interface and come out to initial screen.  

Step9: Now provide the form name mentioned above in the initial screen. 

Step10:  Click on change button and following screen appears. 

Delete the unnecessary empty folder from the context menu.

Now, 

Step11: Go to layout tab, 

Step12: Now go to properties tab, 

When you try to activate the form, it gives some error message as ‘Unknown Layout Type’.

Change the layout type to Standard Layout as shown. 

Activate the form and execute it  

Step13: The output would be

entry.title: Uploading External File in Adobe Forms
entry.content:
UPLOADING EXTERNAL FILE IN ADOBE FORMS 

Uploading External file : 

Scenario: 

A company needs to process vendor invoices in the SAP system, and as part of the Invoice Verification process, employees need to upload supporting documents (such as PDF invoices or scanned images) for audit and payment processing. These documents are often required to be attached to the invoice in SAP for review, verification, and audit purposes. 

In this scenario, Adobe Forms is used to facilitate the upload of the vendor's invoice document directly into the SAP system during the invoice verification process. 

Advantages of Uploading external file in adobe forms: 

  • Reduce Manual Errors 
  • Automatic Document Linking 
  • User-Friendly Interface 
  • Reduce paper usage 
  • Time Saving 

Disadvantages of uploading external file in adobe forms: 

  • Complexity in implementation 
  • System performance and Storage issues 
  • Limited File  type support 
  • Dependence on Adobe and SAP Versions 
  • Cost of Adobe License and Tools  

Here I'm going to upload a below Form. 

Note: File should be PDF Format.

Steps to Upload External file in Adobe forms: 

Step 1: Steps to create Interface: 

  • Goto SFP. 

  • Select the Interface radio button. 

  • Provide Interface name and click on create. 

  • Provide description , Interface type and click on save. 

  • Provide the fields based on your form. 

Step 2: Steps to create Form: 

  • Goto SFP. 

  • Select the Form radio button. 

  • Provide form name and click on create. 

  • Provide description and Interface name and click on save. 

  • Drag and Drop the fields into Adobe form area the fields which  is required for form. 

  • Click on layout for designing the form. 

  • Go to Tools in menu bar. 

  • Click Import node and select the file/template and click on open. 

  • Select the import options based on your requirement and click on Ok. 

  • The template/file is uploaded. 

  • Map the fields from your interface to your temple. 

  • Select the Data view tab in template, it will display the interface parameters. 

  • Mapping(Drag and drop) the parameters based on your field names in form. 

  • Place the parameters to the appropriate place. 
  • If you want to place/mention the same form field name or you want to apply any font styles/Formatting style you can select Object node under Palette menu. 

  • If you want to make any adjustments to fields with the help of Object you can provide. 

  • Adjust the importing/interface parameters based on form fields. 

  • Click on Save. 

  • Active the Form and click on Execute. 
  • Provide the input values and click on execute. 

  • Provide output device type as LPO1 and click on Print Preview.

entry.title: Efficient Data Migration Using CDS Views in SAP S/4HANA
entry.content:
Data migration is an essential part of transitioning to SAP S/4HANA. CDS (Core Data Services) Views provide an efficient, declarative approach to transform and migrate data while leveraging database capabilities like filtering, aggregation, and parallel processing. This blog demonstrates a real-world scenario for data migration using CDS views, complete with code and output, along with a detailed explanation.

This blog explains how to migrate data using CDS views with complete code, an example scenario, and a step-by-step explanation of how it works.

Scenario: Migrating Sales Data

The organization has sales data in the legacy table ZSALES_OLD. The task is to:

  1. Migrate sales data within a specified date range to the target table ZSALES_NEW.
  2. Filter data dynamically using CDS views.
  3. Use AMDP to process data in chunks for optimal performance.

Step-by-Step Implementation

Step 1: Define Source and Target Tables

Legacy Table: ZSALES_OLD

This table holds the sales data to be migrated.

Sales ID Customer ID Sales Date Product ID Quantity Amount
1001CUST012024-01-05PROD00110500
1002CUST022024-01-15PROD0025250
1003CUST032024-02-01PROD003201000
Target Table: ZSALES_NEW

This table will hold the migrated and filtered sales data.

 

@EndUserText.label: 'Sales Data Target Table'
@AbapCatalog.tableCategory: #TRANSPARENT
@AbapCatalog.deliveryClass: #A
@AbapCatalog.dataMaintenance: #ALLOWED
define table ZSALES_NEW {
  key sales_id      : abap.numc(10),
      customer_id   : abap.char(10),
      sales_date    : abap.dats,
      product_id    : abap.char(10),
      quantity      : abap.int4,
      amount        : abap.dec(15,2)
}

 

Step 2: Create CDS View for Data Transformation

CDS views simplify the extraction and transformation of data. Using parameters, the view filters data dynamically during runtime.

 

@AbapCatalog.sqlViewName: 'ZCDS_MIGRATE_SALES'
@EndUserText.label: 'CDS View for Sales Data Migration'
define view ZCDS_MIGRATE_SALES
  with parameters 
    p_start_date : abap.dats,
    p_end_date   : abap.dats
  as select from ZSALES_OLD
{
  sales_id,
  customer_id,
  sales_date,
  product_id,
  quantity,
  amount
}
where sales_date between :p_start_date and :p_end_date

 

How it Works :
The CDS view uses the parameters p_start_date and p_end_date to filter rows within a specified date range. At runtime, these parameters are dynamically supplied.

Step 3: Implement AMDP for Parallel Processing

AMDP allows executing SQLScript directly in the SAP HANA database for enhanced performance. Here, it is used to process the data in chunks.

AMDP Class Definition  

 

CLASS zcl_data_migration DEFINITION
  PUBLIC
  FINAL
  CREATE PUBLIC.

  PUBLIC SECTION.
    INTERFACES: if_amdp_marker_hdb.

    CLASS-METHODS: migrate_sales_data
      IMPORTING
        VALUE(iv_start_date) TYPE dats
        VALUE(iv_end_date)   TYPE dats
        VALUE(iv_chunk_size) TYPE int4.
ENDCLASS.

 

AMDP Class Implementation

 

CLASS zcl_data_migration IMPLEMENTATION.

  METHOD migrate_sales_data BY DATABASE PROCEDURE
    FOR HDB
    LANGUAGE SQLSCRIPT
    USING ZCDS_MIGRATE_SALES ZSALES_NEW.

    DECLARE v_start_date DATE := :iv_start_date;
    DECLARE v_end_date DATE := :iv_end_date;
    DECLARE v_chunk_size INT := :iv_chunk_size;
    DECLARE v_total_days INT;

    -- Calculate total days
    v_total_days := DATEDIFF(DAY, v_start_date, v_end_date) + 1;

    -- Loop through chunks
    FOR i IN 0 .. FLOOR(v_total_days / v_chunk_size) DO
      INSERT INTO ZSALES_NEW
      SELECT *
      FROM ZCDS_MIGRATE_SALES(
        p_start_date => ADD_DAYS(v_start_date, i * v_chunk_size),
        p_end_date   => ADD_DAYS(v_start_date, (i + 1) * v_chunk_size - 1)
      );
    END FOR;

  END METHOD.

ENDCLASS.

 

Code Explaination

 

DECLARE v_start_date DATE := :iv_start_date;
DECLARE v_end_date DATE := :iv_end_date;
DECLARE v_chunk_size INT := :iv_chunk_size;
DECLARE v_total_days INT;

 

  • v_start_date and v_end_date: Store the start and end dates for migration. These are passed from the ABAP layer as input parameters (iv_start_date and iv_end_date).
  • v_chunk_size: Determines the size of each chunk (number of days in each batch).
  • v_total_days: Calculates the total number of days between the start and end dates.

Calculating Total Days

 

v_total_days := DATEDIFF(DAY, v_start_date, v_end_date) + 1;

 

 The DATEDIFF function calculates the number of days between v_start_date and v_end_date. Adding 1 ensures that both boundary dates are included.

Chunked Data Processing

 

FOR i IN 0 .. FLOOR(v_total_days / v_chunk_size) DO

 

FOR loop: Iterates over the data in chunks.

  • The range 0 .. FLOOR(v_total_days / v_chunk_size) ensures each chunk is processed.
  • FLOOR ensures the loop operates with an integer number of chunks.

Data Insertion into Target Table

 

INSERT INTO ZSALES_NEW
SELECT *
FROM ZCDS_MIGRATE_SALES(
  p_start_date => ADD_DAYS(v_start_date, i * v_chunk_size),
  p_end_date   => ADD_DAYS(v_start_date, (i + 1) * v_chunk_size - 1)
);

 

  • INSERT INTO ZSALES_NEW: Inserts data into the target table.
  • SELECT * FROM ZCDS_MIGRATE_SALES: Fetches data from the CDS view based on the chunked date range.
    • ADD_DAYS(v_start_date, i * v_chunk_size): Calculates the start date for the current chunk.
    • ADD_DAYS(v_start_date, (i + 1) * v_chunk_size - 1): Calculates the end date for the current chunk.

This logic ensures that only a subset of the data (one chunk) is processed in each iteration, reducing the load on the database.

How it Works:

  1. Input Parameters:

    • The ABAP layer passes iv_start_date, iv_end_date, and iv_chunk_size as inputs to the method.

  2. Processing:

    • The method calculates the total number of days between the start and end dates.
    • It divides the data into chunks based on the specified iv_chunk_size.

  3. Execution:

    • For each chunk, the method fetches data from the CDS view for the specified date range.
    • The data is then inserted into the target table.

  4. Output:

    • The target table (ZSALES_NEW) contains the migrated data.

Advantages

  1. Scalability:

    • By processing data in chunks, the method can handle large datasets efficiently.

  2. Reuse:

    • The CDS view ZCDS_MIGRATE_SALES can be reused for other scenarios.

  3. Flexibility:

    • The chunk size (iv_chunk_size) can be adjusted dynamically based on performance requirements.

Step 4: Create an ABAP Report to Execute Migration

This report allows you to execute the migration process.

 

REPORT zsales_data_migration.

PARAMETERS: p_start_date TYPE dats,
            p_end_date   TYPE dats,
            p_chunk_size TYPE int4 DEFAULT 10.

START-OF-SELECTION.

  TRY.
      zcl_data_migration=>migrate_sales_data(
        EXPORTING
          iv_start_date = p_start_date
          iv_end_date   = p_end_date
          iv_chunk_size = p_chunk_size
      ).
      WRITE: / 'Data migration completed successfully!'.
    CATCH cx_amdp_error INTO DATA(lx_error).
      WRITE: / 'Data migration failed:', lx_error->get_text( ).
  ENDTRY.

 

Step 5: Execute and Verify Output

 

Sales ID   Customer ID   Sales Date   Product ID   Quantity   Amount
----------------------------------------------------------------------
1001       CUST01        2024-01-05  PROD001      10         500.00
1002       CUST02        2024-01-15  PROD002      5          250.00
1003       CUST03        2024-02-01  PROD003      20         1000.00

 

Conclusion

Data migration is a vital process for successful system upgrades or transitions. Using SAP technologies like CDS Views and AMDP allows for efficient, scalable, and performant data migrations. By automating the process and leveraging parallel processing, you can minimize downtime and ensure data integrity in the target system.

entry.title: End-to-End Guide to Consume RAP application to Fiori Using SAP Business Application Studio
entry.content:
To consume a RAP (RESTful ABAP Programming) application in SAP Fiori using SAP Business Application Studio (BAS), you will typically follow a series of steps involving the creation of the RAP service, exposing it as a Cloud RAP service, and integrating it into a Fiori application. Below is an end-to-end guide outlining these steps. 

 Prerequisites 

Create one RAP application (Managed/Unmanaged/Unmanaged & Save). 

Step-by-Step Guide 

 Login to your BTP account, check if there is any destination, if not Create a destination to Cloud RAP   service. 

 Login to BTP account, Click on SAP Business Application Studio (BAS). 

  Provide the credentials (BTP Credentials), Click on Continue. 

 Click on OK Create Dev Space. 

 Click on New Project from Template. 

  Select SAP Fiori generator template 

  Select the template, and click on Next 

  Select the Data source which you want to create Destination. 

  Select the System (ABAP environment on SAP BTP). 

  Here there is no ABAP Environments, it requires credentials to do this follow the bellow steps. 

  Click three dots (bellow left side), select Cloud Foundry. 

  Click on Services, and login required,  

  Select the Credentials radio button and provide your username (your BTP account mail) and Password. 

 Go back and check the destination. 

  Provide Cloud foundry Details 

  Select Cloud foundry Organization (System generated) 

  Select your Cloud foundry space. 

  Organization and space have been set successfully. 

  Refresh the page. 

 Now select the ABAP Environment and your RAP Service, then Click on Next. 

 Select the Main entity and Navigation Entity and table type as responsive, then click on next. 

 Fill in the required fields, click on finish. 

 Right click on webapp, below the folder. Click on preview application. 

 Select the start Fiori run test. 

  Perform modify operations. 

 

Conclusion 

By following this guide, you will have created and consumed a RAP service using SAP Business Application Studio for a Fiori application. This approach leverages the power of SAP Fiori’s UI5 framework, combined with the RAP model for backend development, making it possible to efficiently create modern SAP Fiori apps.

entry.title: Layoutoriented storage control - change/skip intermediate storage type
entry.content:
Hi all,

 

in our scenario we use layoutoriented storage control to transport HUs from warehouse rack to destination bin via AGV. With confirmation of first warehouse task to intermediate storage type/bin, the second WT is submitted via interface to AGV. AGV then confirms second task. This process is working fine.

In case some/or all of the AGVs are not working, there might be the need to bring the HUs manually with forklift to destination storage bin.

  • Is it possible to change the intermediate storage type in the process or skip the intermediate storage bin and directly confirm to destination storage bin via RF-device? 
  • With confirmation to intermediate bin, WT has status "WT to Subsystem". Is it somehow possible to process the WT in RF-device without changing the status (untick "WT to Subsystem") in monitor first? (or is it possible to change the status in RF?)

Thanks!

entry.title: SCIM User Deployment to IBP System, Role assignment via SAML token possible?
entry.content:
Hello,

We are using SAP IPS do deploy and authorize Users in SAP IBP.

The IBP users receive the role assignment through the SCIM deployment from the IAS, not from a SAML token that is transferred during login.
This presents us with the challenge that the search string in the IPS “aad.group.filter” becomes longer and longer the more roles there are in the IBP which need to be mapped to AD Roles, which slows down the graph query and makes administration more difficult.
Is it possible to assign roles to users via a SAML token when logging into the IBP?
In this case, we would only deploy the users to the IBP via the IPS and thus only have one role in the “aad.group.filter”.

best regards

Bastian

entry.title: Bitcoin - The new Treasury Asset
entry.content:
Over time, balance sheet valuation has become much less important than the income statement when analyzing companies - you could say that it has become irrelevant.

The Classic: Companies that have reached the break-even point pursue a strategy of decapitalizing themselves by distributing virtually all their profits to shareholders, e.g. in the form of dividends or buybacks. Before distributing excess cash, a limited amount can be invested in growth and acquisitions, but saving up or investing randomly is basically always the least attractive option from the shareholders' point of view.

The reason for this is that conservative low-risk positions like cash or treasury bills are dilutive and not worth holding. Even keeping pace with the consumer price index is not enough: Purchasing power should increase, not just keep up.

Why things are changing: The downside of this non-liquidity strategy is that it becomes unnecessarily difficult to switch to large investments such as AI or to manage crises, as we saw in the 2008 financial crisis or the 2020 pandemic. The lack of a large corporate savings position reduces flexibility and adaptability.

Outliers like Apple are not so successful because of their unusually high cash balances, they are able to hold so much cash because they are disproportionately successful. Coca Cola, for example, like many others, has negative tangible equity but can get by with this leveraged position due to its constant cash flows.

Macroeconomic conditions: Raising unstable economic factors like inflation, interest rates and geopolitical risks are making companies changing their mind.

Bitcoin is now an attractive new option for corporate treasuries: no long-term dilution, a 21 million cap - but the average return on this 15-year-old emerging asset class far outstrips the client price index. Wall Street got into bitcoin in 2024, and Blackrock has pulled off one of the most successful ETF launches in ETF history with its iShares Bitcoin Trust ETF, and institutions are buying it.

Shareholders are once again interested in building savings positions when their respective potential returns are in the double-digit annual average. Very important functionality is also Bitcoin as hedge against fiscal deficits, geopolitical risks and currency debasement. Volatility is still high, but a time horizon of more than 4+ years balances out the risk so far.

 
 

 

The First Mover: Business intelligence company MicroStrategy (MSTR) has even switched to a full Bitcoin treasury strategy and has been so successful that even AI-powered Nvidia (NVDA), with 2600% growth from the launch of its Bitcoin treasury strategy (August 2020) to today (November 2024), cannot keep up.

Who goes first: The largest or fastest growing companies have no incentive to pioneer this new, but also volatile, treasury strategy and adopt new paradigms. The more the respective industry stagnates for a forward-thinking company, the more interesting it becomes to drop the decapitalization strategy and switch to building a strong treasury savings in Bitcoin. The company's balance sheet and access to capital markets are key to this adjustment.

The market immediately reacts very positively to Bitcoin treasury announcements, which can be seen in the respective share prices. In the early stages, it is very likely that certain equity and bond managers will take notice of the company, even if the bitcoin treasury exposure is lower. Microsoft will hold a board meeting with MicroStrategy CEO Michael Saylor in December 2024 to take a look at Bitcoin treasury exposure due to shareholder demand.

Action: Game theory says that if you're a fast mover now, the potential return of a Bitcoin Treasury adoption increases with huge opportunities for the economic power of the early companies.

 

Contact at mirko.valentic@sap.com


Learn more:

Navigating the Generative AI Era: Blockchain is key for resilient cross-company communication

NFT – Digital Asset Representation on a blockchain

entry.title: Re: Variable Pay Eligibility Fields in Design Worksheet
entry.content:
Hi @xavierlegarrec 

Maybe I am doing something wrong, but it did not work the same way for me 😥 I tried with this switch, but the result is that the global eligibility rule is basically ignored.

Here’s an example: my template is set to include inactive employees, but the customer obviously doesn’t want to see employees terminated before the bonus period. My global rule specifies: include only those terminated employees with a termination date on or after 1/1/2024. With the switch turned off, I still see employees terminated in 2022 and 2023 on the worksheet—employees who are not eligible for the payout but are still included in the table.

That’s not what I want. So again, the only method that worked for me was to use the Manager Eligibility Rule to exclude them. My tests indicate that if we want to include only selected ineligible employees, the switch needs to be set to ‘false’ in combination with Manager Eligibility Rules.
